cluster.title: Oil prices surge amid concerns over supply disruptions in the Middle East.

synthesis.narrative:
## Iran War Triggers Global Market Turmoil as Energy Infrastructure Comes Under Attack

A rapidly expanding conflict between the U.S./Israel and Iran has sent global markets into freefall and energy prices soaring as the war spreads across the Middle East and disrupts critical oil and gas infrastructure.

### Conflict Timeline and Scope

The conflict began over the weekend with U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran [2][6][7]. President Trump indicated the operation, dubbed "Operation Epic Fury," is designed to last four to five weeks [4][6]. Within 72 hours, the war has directly involved at least 11 countries [6], with Iran launching retaliatory strikes across the region including the UAE, Bahrain, Kuwait, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, Oman, and Iraq [6].

### Energy Infrastructure Under Attack

Iranian strikes have targeted critical energy facilities across the Gulf region. Qatar Energy shut down production at its Ras Laffan plant following "military attacks" [2], while Saudi Arabia's state-run Aramco shuttered its giant Ras Tanura refinery after it was set ablaze in a drone strike [2]. On Monday, debris from Iranian drones struck an Aramco oil refinery in Saudi Arabia [6]. Qatar suspended liquefied natural gas production and other energy products [4], sending European natural gas prices up a stunning 40% [4].

### Strait of Hormuz Effectively Closed

Iran has effectively shut down the strategically vital Strait of Hormuz, through which 20% of the world's oil and gas transits [2]. An Iranian Revolutionary Guards official declared the strait "closed" and warned forces would "set fire to anyone who tries to pass" [2]. While the U.S. military's Central Command has disputed Iran's claim that the strait is officially closed [3], shipping traffic has ground to a near halt [5]. At least five tankers have been damaged, two personnel killed, and about 150 ships are stranded around the strait [5].

### Insurance Crisis Paralyzes Shipping

Major maritime insurers including Gard, Skuld, NorthStandard, the London P&I Club and the American Club have canceled war risk coverage for vessels operating in the Middle East, with cancellations taking effect March 5 [3][5]. This insurance withdrawal has effectively paralyzed shipping even without a physical blockade, as shipping lines are unwilling to expose vessels to risk and insurers won't cover the exposure [2].

### Record-Breaking Shipping Costs

The benchmark freight rate for Very Large Crude Carriers from the Middle East to China hit an all-time high of $423,736 per day on Monday, marking a 94% increase from Friday [3]. The cost of leasing a tanker to ship from the Middle East to China doubled to $400,000 a day [2].

### Global Market Meltdown

Global stock markets tumbled as investors retreated from uncertainty [1]. Japan's Nikkei 225 Index ended Tuesday down more than 3% [2], Korea's Kospi plummeted 7% [4], and European markets saw significant declines with Germany's DAX down 4% and London's FTSE 100 down 2.8% [2]. U.S. futures indicated the S&P 500 would drop 2% at opening, with Dow futures pointing to a 970-point drop [4].

### Energy Price Surge

Brent crude oil rose to $80 a barrel [2], with U.S. crude trading 7.5% higher and bringing total increases since Sunday to more than 13% [4]. Natural gas prices jumped 30% to $1.97 per therm [2], with European natural gas futures rising 40% [4]. U.S. retail gasoline prices have jumped 14 cents since last week to $3.077 per gallon, with analysts predicting prices could reach $3.10-$3.20 per gallon by week's end [4]. Analysts warn oil could surpass $100 a barrel if disruptions continue [2].

### Broader Economic Implications

The conflict is raising fears about inflation and interest rates [2], with expectations that higher fuel costs will feed through to all goods movement by sea as carriers hike rates globally [2]. About 10% of the world's container ships are ensnared in the broader backups, and cargo could soon start piling up at ports in Europe and Asia [5].

citations.title: Global Markets Tumble as Iran War Intensifies
citations.quote: Stocks and bonds slumped as investors retrenched from the uncertain and unpredictable effects that the fighting could have on the world economy.

citations.title: Iran conflict: Global oil, gas prices surge on supply disruption fears
citations.quote: The price of Brent crude oil rose to $80 a barrel and the price of natural gas jumped 30% to $1.97 per therm on Tuesday after Iran effectively shut the key Strait of Hormuz shipping lane, with an official threatening its forces would 'set fire to anyone who tries to pass.'

citations.title: Oil supertanker rates hit all-time high as insurers drop war risk protection in the Middle East
citations.quote: The benchmark freight rate for Very Large Crude Carriers (VLCCs) — used to ship 2 million barrels of oil from the Middle East to China — hit an all-time high of $423,736 per day on Monday, data from LSEG showed. That marked an increase of more than 94% from Friday's close.

citations.title: Oil surges, stocks plunge as fears of prolonged Iran war hit markets
citations.quote: The reaction came as President Donald Trump indicated the U.S.-Israeli operation may last weeks. Iran's retaliatory attacks across the Middle East have hit U.S. embassies and Gulf oil facilities and brought shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, a key waterway for global fuel supplies, to a near standstill.

citations.title: Maritime insurers cancel war risk cover in Gulf: Will it spike energy cost?
citations.quote: At least five tankers have been damaged, two personnel killed and about 150 ships stranded around the strait.

citations.title: Earthquake in the Gulf: Iran war expands to dozen countries in 72 hours
citations.quote: Just 72 hours after the U.S. and Israel began bombing Iran, the war has already consumed nearly the entire Middle East, reached the gates of Europe and raised new fears of attacks on American soil.

citations.title: The U.S. War on Iran Rattles Energy Markets
citations.quote: Oil prices jumped significantly on Monday, the third day of the U.S. war on Iran, with a roller-coaster rise of between 7 percent and 9 percent in the price of benchmark crude oil, as Tehran widened the war to include targeting energy facilities all around the region.

outlierPerspectives: Foreign Policy's analysis questioning why oil prices aren't higher given the severity of the situation, comparing current prices unfavorably to 2008 levels despite more severe disruptions
Axios's emphasis on the conflict's rapid 72-hour expansion across 11+ countries as historically unprecedented
Foreign Policy's detailed explanation of how insurance market dynamics (rather than physical blockades) are the primary mechanism closing shipping routes
missingContext: Historical precedent for similar conflicts and their duration/resolution patterns
Strategic petroleum reserve levels and release mechanisms in major consuming countries
Alternative shipping routes and their capacity to handle diverted traffic
Iran's actual military capabilities versus its threats regarding Strait closure
Diplomatic efforts or back-channel communications to de-escalate
Impact on global supply chains beyond energy (food, manufacturing goods)
Regional allies' military capabilities and willingness to intervene
Domestic political pressures in involved countries affecting decision-making

deepAnalysis.historicalContext: This conflict echoes the 1980s 'Tanker War' during the Iran-Iraq conflict when both sides attacked oil infrastructure and shipping, leading to U.S. naval intervention to protect Gulf shipping. However, the current crisis is more severe in scope, involving direct U.S.-Israeli military action against Iran rather than proxy conflict. The insurance industry's rapid withdrawal of coverage represents an unprecedented commercial response that effectively creates a blockade without physical enforcement. The Strait of Hormuz has been a flashpoint since the 1980s, but this represents the first time Iran has attempted complete closure during active hostilities with the U.S. The global financial integration and just-in-time supply chains of 2024 make the economic impact potentially more severe than historical precedents.
implications: Global supply chain crisis as 10% of container ships are affected and alternative routes add weeks to delivery times
Inflationary pressure worldwide as energy costs feed through to all transported goods
Potential collapse of European manufacturing dependent on Middle East energy supplies
Shift in global energy trade patterns toward non-Middle East suppliers, potentially benefiting U.S., Russia, and African producers
Maritime insurance industry facing massive claims and potential restructuring of war risk coverage globally
Acceleration of energy transition policies as governments seek to reduce Middle East dependency
Potential for broader regional war if conflict spreads to involve other major powers
Central bank policy complications as energy-driven inflation conflicts with economic growth concerns
Strategic petroleum reserve depletion in major consuming nations
Permanent increase in shipping costs and insurance premiums even after conflict resolution
